speaker: interviewer
text: Stop and just tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I'll go and sit down. And that's when we'll really start doing the interview. You, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we go to the second tape, at the beginning, all we'll do is you'll just say this is Nathan Fefer? I got it right, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It takes me a while. This is Nathan Fefer. And it's tape number two. We usually do that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date? You want the date?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've been doing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is June 30, 1995. The survivor is Nathan Fefer. F-E-F-E-R. The interviewer is Debra Kimmel, K-I-M-M-E-L. The interview was being conducted in Deerfield Beach, Florida, USA. The interview will be conducted in English. This is tape number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to say-- do you want to say this stuff here? My name is--

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Deborah Kimmel. Today's June 30, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Nathan Fefer, F-E-F-E-R, in Deerfield Beach, Florida, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. And you can cut. Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Nathan Fefer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-E-F-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the name you were born with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I'm 82.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a small village, Orzenay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-R-Z-E-N-A-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Kovel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: A big city. K-O-V-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have six sisters and one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the names of your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Pasi, Lea, Sarah, Zelda, Libby, and Rachael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Mandel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Melach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fega.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a store, a grocery store. And we had a couple acres land with a barn. And we raise a couple cows. We really made a nice living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town was there about 400 Jewish families. And we had a rabbi. We had two synagogues. And we have a Yeshiva there and a [INAUDIBLE]. It a-- it was nice, you know. We live between Ukrainian people. One side was Ukrainian. And we were in the middle. And on the other side was a Polish village.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Ukrainian people never bothered with us. I used to go 10:00, 12:00 in the night. They never touch us. And we make business with the Ukrainian people. That's it. Did you grow up in a religious home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I keep Shabbas, everything kosher. I keep kosher now here, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what Shabbat was like for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there in Europe? Yeah, we make a nice living, you know. And we all go to school, you know. We make a nice living. Not millionaires, but very good off. You know, we made a nice living.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to when you were young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of student were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think not bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what you were like as a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I like-- I-- I was always, you know with the Zionist organization. And later then, I grow up a little older, I was more on the left side.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you used to do with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we used to go out, you know, dance, you know. It was organizations there. It was a library, a little library. It was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship like with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. I was raised like-- like-- like a king. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know. He was a religious man, but not fanatic. So when I want to do something else, he didn't bother me, see? Like, he-- he didn't see it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like growing up with all those sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was OK. I was the king. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what type of woman your mother was?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a nice, quite woman. She was busy to raise eight kids, you know? She was a very nice, quiet woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what she used to cook on the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Chickens. There's big challahs. I was a bad eater, you know? A very bad eater, even now. So I don't like to eat lox. She used to make special for me matzoh. Just for me to make matzoh.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much older were your sisters than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we were about a year, or two years from each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, had any of your sisters gotten married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Five got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they move away from home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Two, three were in the same village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what that was like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, was-- always in good-- in good friends, you know? We-- we didn't fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you finished public school, did you continue with your studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Yeshiva. Because my father wants me, you know, to learn Talmud and everything, you know. So half a day, it was with Yeshiva. And half of a day, we had private two teachers to teach us Hebrew and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: During those years, did you ever face any kind of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I can't say no. Maybe in the bigger cities. But the [INAUDIBLE], the Ukrainian people, they used to fight with the Polish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: They left us alone. And if something happened, you know, we were fighters. We fight again, you know? Like, it was [INAUDIBLE]. And if some you know, Ukrainian guy was strong, we liked to make a little bit trouble. So we give him back. So he quiet down, you know. We were fighting good. But it was very good. You know, not too bad, you know? When you look back on those early days of being a young boy, what stands out the most?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was good. I was a strong man, you know. I was always in the business, you know. I was the business man from the family. And I enjoyed this, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any grandparents that lived nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I don't see-- I didn't see, because they died before, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never knew your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. One, I remember, like-- like you know, through a sleep. But I can't even recognize, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, many. I lost 70 people from my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the times when-- when you would get together with your aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. You know, like weddings, you know, together, like, in the synagogue, you know. Yeah, we were in good times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you used to spend the Jewish holidays together with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In Europe, it was not like this. Everybody separate, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what Passover was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Passover was a-- was a holiday, you know. They used to make wine. My father bought grapes. You know, and lady squeezes this in a bottle. And it has to stay two weeks, you know. [INAUDIBLE] wine, matzohs, you know. It was a nice, nice Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother make the matzoh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, later in the end it was already machines that they make matzohs. But we bake by hand, you know. Cause you know, if you bake by hand, it's more kosher, like they say, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would be with you during the Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters, the father, the mother. We had a big family, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you go through the service?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there other holidays that stand out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, Passover, [INAUDIBLE], Rosh Hashanah, [INAUDIBLE]. You know, [INAUDIBLE] they used to get drunk. They used the dance in the streets with the Torahs. And if somebody got married, they make the hoop and [INAUDIBLE]. We were not afraid. Nobody touched us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you used to go on dates with girls then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do on those dates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they go around. They talk. They walk. Sometimes a kiss. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you notice that things were starting to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they're starting to change when Hitler came to power, you know. We felt already a little bit, but we didn't know. We know nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: We felt, because there was rumors. Hitler do this. Hitler does this in Germany. But we didn't believe it. Because-- because before the First War, the Germans were good people, you know? So we didn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first time that something directly affected you with these changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, until the war started, until they occupied Poland, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out, he occupied Poland. So he made the deal with-- with Stalin-- two gangsters, you know. He took half Poland. And Hitler took half Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were on the half Poland with the Russian. And half Poland was with the Germans, you know. So with the Russians, they didn't bother us, you know. Did your life change at all with the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah. You could make business, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If you rich, they took away everything, you know. But still, the life, it didn't bother you though. The life was safe, you know. You have to go according their law. That's it. [INAUDIBLE] didn't like it, but no choice, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what those laws were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, no business. If you have a store and merchandise, they took away. And they-- and they sold it, you know. It was another life already. But still, you can go to the synagogue, you know. But it was not so free like before, you know. But the life was secured, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have money at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everybody has the money [INAUDIBLE] the penny. But you've got, you know, merchandise. You sold this, they give you something else, you know. You tried to make a living, you know. And I worked for them, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was manager in a store. And they got a store every farmer used to-- to bring the product there, you know, like, potatoes, feed, this and this. And I have-- and later, they came with trucks. And they took away, you know. So I was working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to your store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. There [INAUDIBLE] sold out. That's it. There was no more store. It was no private there. Everything went to the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you-- did you do this job for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the war broke out. '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: '41 On Sunday-- the first day is Sunday. There was [? mobilization ?] in the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took us-- they took us into the army. And I was-- I never had a rifle in my life. And they gave me a rifle, you know. And we were fighting the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember exactly how they came to get you to join the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a public, in the-- in the-- in the city. This you have to go to the police. And that's it. They took you away, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you went to the police?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me I have to go to register with the army. And that's it. They took me away and put a Russian [INAUDIBLE] with a rifle, with grenades, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the uniform looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they had the Russian uniforms, the blouse, you know, with the boots you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with any of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were, but later we disappeared. Everybody has his own job, you know. But I never saw them again, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to the police station. They gave you the uniform. And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me to the army. There was a-- to the army, and then with the army, and then on the front, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where exactly did they send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 50 miles from my town. It was right on the border with the Germans, you know. They were fighting with the Germans not-- not to long, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went to that spot, did you know anyone who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know from my [INAUDIBLE]. But later we-- and a matter of fact, we [INAUDIBLE] we didn't recognize each other. [LAUGHS] They took the hair off with everything. We didn't recognize each other. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And they handed you a rifle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The rifle. And there was a-- and then I went with them, you know. I remember like, in the night time, they-- they give me a long roll of the telephone wires. And one Russian soldier went with me. And we go near the Germans. And then later when [INAUDIBLE], the leader started shooting. So I was deaf. So he talked to me, I told him I didn't hear nothing. He said, he put in the ears the finger, then you wouldn't hear nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then after two days fighting, the Germans advance. And we-- and we go back, back, back, back. [INAUDIBLE] because the Ukrainian, didn't like the-- the Russian people, the communists. They were against the Russian people. So they have a chance to run away. They left everything on the road. And they run away. So why did the Germans advance? Because the Russians fall apart, you know. Until later, they were-- later in Stalingrad, they picked up. But after two months, they took me in prisoners of war, you know, with the Ukrainian people. Where were you when they took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, you know, near Kiev, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still part of the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But if they collapsed the army, they took everybody. They took me. Took me away. And they put me in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before this, the people who-- who were fighting by your side, were they all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. It was the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything that-- that set the Jewish soldiers apart?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They don't say nothing. In the [? war ?] time, you know-- see, Stalin was very bad. But when the war broke out for the Jews, he open. He says everybody wants to run away to Russia can go away without no papers. Nobody let in every Jew to the country. He did it. He killed later, but he did it. But the Jews did have the same right as everybody in Russia. Thousands and thousands and thousands of Jews saved, because he let them the Jews into Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did it come about that you were taken a prisoner of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, they-- they took around maybe 500, 600 guys. And you have to put your hands up, and that's it. No choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was non-Jews there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me to a prison-- to a-- a prisoner of war, to a jail, in Kovel, a big jail. Then after two weeks, they took us out by trucks and put us to the Chelm concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what those two weeks were like in the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. They didn't bother, no. Everybody was the same. See the Germans, if they didn't have to do it, they wouldn't do it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still wearing the uniform from the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what the physical layout was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was in a good condition. You know, physically good condition.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the-- what did the prison look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the prison. It looks like a prison, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in one big room? Were there small rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small rooms. Everybody has his own room.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had your own room? Did your family know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they took me to the army, they were crying. So they dead, and I'm alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what that was like, saying goodbye to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. They were crying, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the last you heard of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they took me to Chelm camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they took people by trucks, you know. Not just me, but Ukrainian people, too. Everybody, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you left the jail, you were still with Jewish and non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, and non-Jewish, Ukrainian people, and Jewish, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with people who had been from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friendships at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put you in trucks. How long was that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't take too long, probably an hour and a half or two hours. It's not too far, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't know where I was going. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got to your destination?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got there. And they put us in the barracks, you know. It was just a-- [INAUDIBLE] just starting to build a camp. It was there, a camp, a death camp, no food, no nothing, you know. [INAUDIBLE]. And they took us to work, you know. Because every-- every day was 20, 30 dead people from there, from hunger, from death. So they took the Jewish people. We have to take them there to the graves.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from our camp there was about 5 miles, a little wooden area. They dig a big, a big hole with bulldozers. And we used to-- and they made a box with the 2 by 4 on one side, and a 2 by 4 on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And four people was carrying the box with the dead person. Till we reach there, a lot of people, you know, died. Of course, sometimes it was a rainy day. With mud, you couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the guy took a gun and-- and killed him. So till they reached there, was already two three people in one box. This was the worst job in your life, you know. When you first got to this camp and you got off the truck, can you describe exactly what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us off. They put the Ukrainian people separate, the Jewish people separate, the Polish people separate. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they separated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, right away they separated everybody, separated everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do once you were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put me in a barrack there with the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just-- just to sleep on the floor, was nothing there. Even the roof was not finished there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in November, I think was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was no roof over your head?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was cold, rain, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] the blouse from the army just. I was wearing two months, the same blouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing on your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoes. If you have a good pair of shoes, they took away from you. They give you their shoes, you know. Wooden shoes just to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the other Jewish prisoners from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were from Poland, you know. They was from Russia, too, you know. But the most Russian of the prisoners, the Jews, they killed [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A little bit soup, water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how often they gave you the soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, they used to give you like tea, you know, but nothing. And then in the day time, later, for supper they give you a piece of bread with a little bit soup, watery soup. And that's it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what it was-- how they woke you in the morning and how you got off to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: They woke in the morning. Sure, they [INAUDIBLE] work in the morning. They came-- they came here two soldiers. They-- they took you. And you have to go with them to work. And one time, we worked there outside the camp, not too far maybe, across the road. So in the meantime, the Hitler Youth came to this camp, to the Jewish camp. And they start beating the Jews. The Germans [INAUDIBLE] with us, we were about 10 people working there. He was a very nice, good German. He saw what was going on. But 4 o'clock-- it's 4 o'clock, you have to finish the work. But the Germans, a law's a law. It's a law to [INAUDIBLE]. And it's a law to this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he saw what's going on. When he will take us there, we'll get beaten up. But he has no choice. So he told us, clean, clean, clean up good. Clean up the [INAUDIBLE]. Clean up good. Just to take a couple more minutes until this go over. He was a very good, a good German, you know. Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But he knew, he was for sure, you know, we were going to be beaten up there. So he kept us a little bit more, more just to save us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got back that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] a lot of the people was injured, crying. So what are you going to do? Crying. Crying to God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you think about God when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, sometimes yes and sometimes no. If you see they take a rabbi with a tallis and tefilllin, and they kill them, so-- so sometimes you think is no God. And sometimes, yes. And most, I believe in my father. When I have bad times, you know, in sleep when I-- when I saw my father, I knew right away I'll be saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any friends when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not in Chelm. But in another camp, I made-- I made friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about probably 2-- 23 or 24, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of the men a similar age to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. The age where you go in the army, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you-- how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two and a half months, you know. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And then later came a German. He said he wants 100 people, work people, shoemakers, tailors, [INAUDIBLE], carpenters.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said I'm a carpenter. So what are you going to lose? You're going to live there another month. And you dead anyways. So [INAUDIBLE]. During the time that you were there, had other-- had other transports also come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: New transports? Sure. Sure. My [INAUDIBLE] already 39,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any sense of how many of the people who you came in with were still there when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Same camp, everybody lives for himself, you know, just to survive, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have roll call there? Roll call in the morning? Were you counted in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every morning. God forbid one runs away. So he took a line with people. Then one SS man with a stick, and he count 1, 2, 3, like this, with a stick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen on the days where somebody was-- where the number didn't come out right?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were searching around, you know. If they find them, they hung him up. They hung them up. And everybody has to go out and see he's hung. And the Germans stood with a [INAUDIBLE] and you pick things, so then you can turn around the man who hangs on the rope. If you have to look right, God forbid you don't look. You have to look right at the person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a common occurrence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- how many times were you counted per day?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean dead people? Who knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they did they count before you went out to work? And did they count--

speaker: interviewee
text: They count if you [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So twice a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, sure. When I was one day passing the camp to work, I saw one guy. There was a telephone post. He was tied up to the telephone post with ropes and a big carrot in his mouth. And the sun was hot. And it was like this. Probably, he died there. I don't know. I thought he was Jewish, or not Jewish. This I don't know. But I saw him there. That's what they did, you know.
